Olist Sales Structure

What is selling, what is not, and what should Sales do next?

Group 2

2026-06-23

  • Business Question
  • Sales Structure: What Is Selling?
  • What Is Not Selling?
  • Strategic Grouping: Volume vs. Value
  • Long-Tail Risk
  • Advanced Commercial Health Clustering
  • Predictive Model: Anticipating Product Strategic Group
  • Business Recommendations

Business Question

This report answers two sales questions:

  • What is selling? Categories and products with the strongest delivered order volume and revenue.
  • What is not finding customers? Categories and products with very low delivered sales or no completed delivered sales.

The analysis uses only delivered orders to focus on completed purchases. Product-level charts use category names plus short product IDs because the Olist dataset does not include human-readable product names.

Sales Structure: What Is Selling?

Top Categories By Order Quantity

Interpretation: These categories are the strongest observed demand pools. They are the natural candidates for seller coverage, stock availability monitoring, and promotion planning.

Top Categories By Revenue

Interpretation: Revenue leaders should not automatically receive all investment, but they deserve deeper review for margins, freight cost, seller reliability, and repeatability.

Top Individual Products

Interpretation: Winning SKUs can be used as benchmarks for pricing, product content, seller quality, and promotional mechanics.

What Is Not Selling?

Bottom Categories: The Tail

Category Orders Revenue Order Share Revenue Share
Security And Services 2 R$ 283 0.00% 0.00%
Fashion Childrens Clothes 7 R$ 520 0.01% 0.00%
Cds Dvds Musicals 12 R$ 730 0.01% 0.01%

Flowers 29 R$ 1,110 0.03% 0.01%
Pc Gamer 7 R$ 1,307 0.01% 0.01%
Diapers And Hygiene 25 R$ 1,501 0.03% 0.01%
Arts And Craftmanship 23 R$ 1,814 0.02% 0.01%
La Cuisine 13 R$ 2,055 0.01% 0.02%
Fashion Sport 26 R$ 2,095 0.03% 0.02%

Interpretation: The bottom 10 categories together contribute 0.09% of delivered item revenue. This is evidence of weak observed performance, but not proof that customers reject these categories. Low revenue may also reflect limited availability, weak listing quality, high freight, few reviews, or short time on platform.

Interpretation: These are the first candidates for sales operations diagnosis. The issue may be demand, but it may also be cancelled orders, stockouts, inactive sellers, poor search visibility, or weak product pages.

Strategic Grouping: Volume vs. Value

Bucket Category Orders Revenue / Order Revenue
High Volume - High Value Health Beauty 8,647 R$ 143 R$ 1,233,132
High Volume - High Value Watches Gifts 5,495 R$ 212 R$ 1,166,177
High Volume - High Value Sports Leisure 7,530 R$ 127 R$ 954,853
High Volume - Low Value Bed Bath Table 9,272 R$ 110 R$ 1,023,435
High Volume - Low Value Furniture Decor 6,307 R$ 113 R$ 711,928
High Volume - Low Value Housewares 5,743 R$ 107 R$ 615,629
Low Volume - High Value Computers 177 R$ 1,236 R$ 218,684
Low Volume - High Value Home Appliances 2 227 R$ 476 R$ 107,954
Low Volume - High Value Agro Industry And Commerce 177 R$ 399 R$ 70,566
Low Volume - Low Value Art 195 R$ 121 R$ 23,657
Low Volume - Low Value Fashion Shoes 235 R$ 98 R$ 23,001
Low Volume - Low Value Food Drink 221 R$ 68 R$ 14,943

This table now shows the top 3 revenue categories in each strategic bucket, so all four groups are represented.

High Volume - High Value categories are the strongest commercial candidates because they combine scale and value.

High Volume - Low Value categories are traffic drivers. Sales can use them to grow order frequency, cross-sell, bundle accessories, and negotiate efficient fulfillment.

Low Volume - High Value categories are revenue anchors. Sales should protect seller quality, product content, delivery reliability, and margin before pushing aggressive volume growth.

Low Volume - Low Value categories are the clearest candidates for listing diagnosis, bundling tests, or rationalisation.

Long-Tail Risk

Interpretation: A broad catalog can improve customer choice, but the tail should be managed deliberately. Low-selling products may need better listing content, bundling, pricing tests, or retirement if they add operational complexity without strategic value.

Advanced Commercial Health Clustering

The median split above is easy to explain, but it imposes fixed thresholds. The clustering analysis below uses the actual distribution of volume, revenue, and revenue per order to discover more data-driven strategic groups.

Optimal Number of Clusters

K-means vs. PAM Clustering

PAM Cluster Profile

PAM_Cluster Total Categories Avg Units Sold Avg Revenue Avg Revenue / Order
1 26 339 R$ 67,023 R$ 262
2 26 103 R$ 7,238 R$ 91
3 22 4,486 R$ 513,214 R$ 130

Interpretation: PAM is useful for business discussion because each cluster is anchored around real category medoids rather than abstract centroids. The profile table should be read as a data-driven segmentation of commercial health, not as a final decision rule: categories in lower-performing clusters should still be reviewed for price, stock, listing quality, seller reliability, and freight cost before action is taken.

Predictive Model: Anticipating Product Strategic Group

This section builds a Random Forest classifier to predict the PAM commercial-health cluster of a product from its physical characteristics and pricing. The business use case is early screening: when a new product enters the platform, Sales can estimate which strategic group it most resembles.

##          Actual
## Predicted    1    2    3
##         1   97    2   34
##         2    0   53    1
##         3  889  267 8360
Overall accuracy: 87.7%
Cluster Precision Recall
1 72.9% 9.8%
2 98.1% 16.5%
3 87.9% 99.6%

Interpretation: This model should be treated as a screening tool, not a final sales decision engine. If accuracy is acceptable, the feature-importance chart helps Sales understand whether early product signals such as price, weight, description length, or photo count are useful proxies for strategic potential. If accuracy is weak, it suggests that product attributes alone are not enough and should be complemented with seller quality, stock availability, reviews, delivery performance, and marketing exposure.

Business Recommendations

  • Defend the winners: keep top-volume and top-revenue categories in stock, maintain strong seller coverage, and monitor delivery promises so proven demand is not lost to operational issues.
  • Bundle traffic drivers: use High Volume - Low Value categories for bundles, add-ons, and cross-sell campaigns to lift basket value without depending only on discounts.
  • Protect revenue anchors: for Low Volume - High Value categories, focus on seller reliability, content quality, freight economics, and margin before scaling promotions.
  • Diagnose the tail: review bottom categories and no-delivered-sale products for price, freight, stock history, images, descriptions, reviews, and search visibility before discontinuing them.
  • Rationalise dead stock carefully: products with no delivered sales should be grouped into improve, promote, bundle, or retire decisions based on operational evidence, not sales volume alone.
